edges.description: Viral exposures may induce local islet interferon programs that make beta cells more immunogenic and stressed.
intermediate_mechanisms: Beta-cell viral RNA sensing and local interferon signaling

edges.description: Upper-intestinal viral infection can reprogram shared draining lymph-node immune traffic toward pancreatic islet inflammation in a mouse model.
intermediate_mechanisms: Migratory dendritic-cell and beta-cell-reactive T-cell inflammatory polarization

edges.description: Pro-inflammatory shared lymph-node crosstalk may increase pancreatic islet lymphocyte infiltration and accelerate beta-cell autoimmunity.
intermediate_mechanisms: Beta-cell-reactive T-cell inflammatory polarization in shared draining lymph nodes

edges.description: Interferon-exposed beta cells may amplify autoimmune recognition through antigen-presentation and chemokine programs.
Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells leads to absolute or near-absolute insulin deficiency.

edges.source: ER Stress and Unfolded Protein Response

nodes.description: The immune system mistakenly targets and destroys insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas through T cell-mediated cytotoxicity and autoantibody production.

evidence.reference_title: Islet abnormalities in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is a T-cell-mediated autoimmune disease that results in the destruction of the insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreatic islets of Langerhans.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiological mechanisms involving aggressive islet cell destruction in fulminant type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Phenotypes of infiltrating cells around and/or into islets were mainly dendritic cells, macrophages and CD8+ T cells.

evidence.reference_title: Immune and Pancreatic β Cell Interactions in Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The autoimmune destruction of the pancreatic islet beta cells is due to a targeted lymphocyte attack.

evidence.reference_title: Life and death of β cells in Type 1 diabetes: A comprehensive review.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is an autoimmune disorder characterized by the destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes results from the destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells by a beta cell-specific autoimmune process.

evidence.reference_title: Partners in Crime: Beta-Cells and Autoimmune Responses Complicit in Type 1 Diabetes Pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is an autoimmune disease characterized by autoreactive T cell-mediated destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta-cells.

evidence.reference_title: Regulatory B Cells in Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes is an organ-specific autoimmune disease characterized by immune-mediated beta cell destruction in pancreatic islets.

evidence.reference_title: Bioengineering strategies for inducing tolerance in autoimmune diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune disease marked by the destruction of insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreatic islets.

nodes.description: Persistent hyperglycemia over time can lead to damage in various tissues and organs.

evidence.reference_title: New insights into the mechanisms of diabetic complications: role of lipids and lipid metabolism.
evidence.snippet: Hyperglycaemia, dyslipidaemia and hypertension combine with the duration and type of diabetes to define the distinct pathophysiology underlying diabetic kidney disease, diabetic retinopathy and diabetic neuropathy.

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 Diabetes and Associated Cardiovascular Damage: Contribution of Extracellular Vesicles in Tissue Crosstalk.
evidence.snippet: Hyperglycemia is the common denominator for most of the chronic diabetic vascular complications, which represent the main cause of life reduction in T1D patients.

evidence.reference_title: Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus: pathophysiology.
evidence.snippet: The remaining 15 to 20% of patients have insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, a disorder caused by the destruction of insulin-producing endocrine cells within the pancreas and currently considered to be the result of an autoimmune process. During the course of both types of diabetes mellitus, the so-called long-term complications of diabetes invariably occur to some extent in all patients. These complications include retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy, and premature atherosclerosis.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of Epigenetic Modifications in Late Complications in Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The prevention of hyperglycemia is very important to avoid or at least postpone the development of micro- and macrovascular complications, also known as late complications. These include diabetic retinopathy, chronic renal failure, diabetic neuropathy, and cardiovascular diseases.

evidence.reference_title: [Diabetic ketoacidosis].
evidence.snippet: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an emergency medical condition that can be life-threatening if not treated properly. Diabetic ketoacidosis occurs most often in patients with type 1 diabetes (formerly called insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus); however, its occurrence in patients with type 2 diabetes (formerly called noninsulin-dependent diabetes mellitus) is not as rare as was once thought.

evidence.reference_title: Brittle type 1 diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: A small group of patients affected by type 1 diabetes mellitus is characterized by a severe instability of glycemic values with frequent and unpredictable hypoglycemic and/or ketoacidosis episodes which cannot be explained by errors of patients or diabetologists.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: The article points out the signs and symptoms to be aware of when the person is in the acute state of diabetic ketoacidosis, hyperglycemic hyperosmolar nonketotic coma (or state), and severe hypoglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetic ketoacidosis as a unique initial presentation of cystic fibrosis.
evidence.snippet: Diabetic ketoacidosis as a unique initial presentation of cystic fibrosis.

nodes.description: Life-threatening metabolic emergency from insulin deficiency
Inflammatory cytokines induce endoplasmic reticulum stress in beta cells, activating the unfolded protein response and contributing to beta cell dysfunction and apoptosis.
nodes.id: ER Stress and Unfolded Protein Response

evidence.reference_title: Interferons are key cytokines acting on pancreatic islets in type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The IFN-induced gene signatures have a strong correlation with those observed in beta cells from individuals with type 1 diabetes, and the level of expression of specific IFN-stimulated genes is positively correlated with proteins present in islets of these individuals, regulating beta cell responses to 'danger signals' such as viral infections.

nodes.description: Viral infections or early dietary factors (cow's milk, gluten) may trigger or accelerate the autoimmune response in genetically susceptible individuals.

evidence.reference_title: Environmental factors in the pathogenesis of type 1 diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Various exogenous triggers, such as certain dietary factors and viruses, are thought to induce the autoimmune process leading in some individuals to extensive beta-cell destruction and ultimately to the clinical manifestation of type 1 diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Protection against or triggering of Type 1 diabetes? Different roles for viral infections.
evidence.snippet: The ability to induce strong cellular immune responses and to cause inflammation in the target organ makes viral infections prime candidates for the initiation of islet autoreactivity.

evidence.reference_title: The CVB and etiology of type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The most often cited environmental agents implicated as initiators of T1D are the human enteroviruses, in particular the group B coxsackieviruses (CVB).

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of viral-induced diabetes.
evidence.snippet: dmsee@uci.edu Serologic case-control studies have suggested an association between coxsasckie group B viruses and insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM)

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus and Celiac Disease: Distinct Autoimmune Disorders That Share Common Pathogenic Mechanisms.
evidence.snippet: Viral infections and early exposure to gluten or cow''s milk in the infant diet have been implicated in disease pathogenesis.

nodes.description: Certain HLA gene variants (HLA-DQ2, HLA-DQ8) and other susceptibility genes (INS) increase the risk of developing Type I Diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Genetics of type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The HLA-DQ genes are the primary susceptibility genes within this region, although other genes may also contribute. The IDDM2 locus maps to a variable number of tandem repeats in the insulin gene region on chromosome 11.

evidence.reference_title: HLA-DQA1 and HLA-DQB1 Alleles, Conferring Susceptibility to Celiac Disease and Type 1 Diabetes, are More Expressed Than Non-Predisposing Alleles and are Coordinately Regulated.
evidence.snippet: HLA DQA1*05 and DQB1*02 alleles encoding the DQ2.5 molecule and HLA DQA1*03 and DQB1*03 alleles encoding DQ8 molecules are strongly associated with celiac disease (CD) and type 1 diabetes (T1D), two common autoimmune diseases (AD)

evidence.reference_title: HLA-DQ beta gene contributes to susceptibility and resistance to insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Over half of the inherited predisposition to insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus maps to the region of chromosome 6 that contains the highly polymorphic HLA class II genes which determine immune responsiveness.

evidence.reference_title: The genetics of HLA-associated disease.
evidence.snippet: Accumulating evidence suggests that MHC susceptibility for T1D is recessive, with susceptibility alleles more common than protective alleles.

evidence.reference_title: Genes influencing innate and acquired immunity in type 1 diabetes and latent autoimmune diabetes in adults.
evidence.snippet: DQ8 and DQ2 are associated with susceptibility to and DQ6 with protection from type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM).

evidence.reference_title: Biomarkers of susceptibility to type 1 diabetes with special reference to the Indian population.
evidence.snippet: Susceptibility to T1D is strongly linked to a major genetic locus that is the major histocompatibility complex (MHC) and several other minor loci including insulin, CTLA4 that contribute to diabetes risk in an epistatic way.

nodes.description: Shared draining lymph nodes for the pancreas and upper small intestine may integrate gut-derived viral inflammation with pancreatic antigen presentation, shifting beta-cell-reactive T cells toward inflammatory phenotypes and increasing islet infiltration.

evidence.reference_title: Lymph node sharing between pancreas, gut, and liver leads to immune crosstalk and regulation of pancreatic autoimmunity.
evidence.snippet: Our study uncovers immune crosstalk through LN co-drainage as a powerful force regulating pancreatic autoimmunity.

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 diabetes-associated HLA-DQ8 transdimer accommodates a unique peptide repertoire.
evidence.snippet: HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8 are strongly predisposing haplotypes for type 1 diabetes (T1D).

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 diabetes-associated HLA-DQ8 transdimer accommodates a unique peptide repertoire.
evidence.snippet: HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8 are strongly predisposing haplotypes for type 1 diabetes (T1D).

evidence.reference_title: The association between the HLA-G 14-bp insertion/deletion polymorphism and type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The DR3-DQ2/DR4-DQ8 genotype conferred the highest detected risk for T1D.

evidence.reference_title: Next Generation Sequencing Identifies the HLA-DQA1*03:03 Allele in the Type 1 Diabetes Risk-Associated HLA-DQ8 Serotype.
evidence.snippet: The combination of HLA-DQA1*03:01 and DQB1*03:02 alleles (summarized as 'HLA-DQ8') is reported to be among the two most prevalent HLA class II haplotypes in Caucasian type 1 diabetes patients.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes: Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes is defined as a state of hyperglycemia due to insulin deficiency caused by autoimmune pancreatic beta-cell destruction.

evidence.reference_title: Multi-level Analysis of HbA1c in Diagnosis and Prognosis of Diabetic Patients.
evidence.snippet: HbA1c offers a trustworthy indicator of chronic hyperglycemia and strongly correlates with the likelihood of long-term consequences from diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of prediabetes.
evidence.snippet: The phenotype of prediabetes includes dyslipidemia and higher arterial blood pressure.

evidence.reference_title: The insulin gene in diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Lack of insulin production or abnormalities affecting insulin secretion are key to the development of almost all forms of diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: The article points out the signs and symptoms to be aware of when the person is in the acute state of diabetic ketoacidosis.

evidence.reference_title: Strong association of insulin gene INS-VNTR polymorphisms with type 1 diabetes in the Romanian population.
evidence.snippet: Our results indicate an exceptionally strong association of the class I INS-VNTR alleles with T1DM for the Romanian population.

evidence.reference_title: Type I insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus: a model for autoimmune polygenic disorders.
evidence.snippet: Genetic susceptibility predominantly results from specific alleles in the HLA complex and insulin gene region.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic linkage and association studies of Type I diabetes: challenges and rewards.
evidence.snippet: However, only within the past decade has it been possible to systematically attempt to identify the genes that increase susceptibility to this disorder using linkage and association analysis of genetic markers distributed across the genome.

evidence.reference_title: The insulin gene in diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Because insulin has such a central role in the pathogenesis of both forms of diabetes, the insulin gene (INS) has always been considered a candidate susceptibility gene.

evidence.reference_title: Concordance for type 1 diabetes in identical twins is affected by insulin genotype.
evidence.snippet: The disease-associated INS genotype (Hph I) was identified in 87.5% of the concordant twins but only in 59.5% (P = 0.005) of the discordant twins.

evidence.reference_title: De-coding genetic risk variants in type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Over half of the genetic risk has been attributed to the human leukocyte antigen (HLA) class II gene region and to the insulin (INS) gene locus.

nodes.description: Without insulin, cells are unable to utilize glucose for energy production, leading to cellular energy deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Challenges for gene therapy of type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 or insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus is caused by autoimmune attack and selective destruction of the pancreatic beta cells.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: The following article reviews the basic pathophysiology of both type 1 diabetes mellitus and type 2 diabetes mellitus as we understand it today.

nodes.description: Insulin deficiency also leads to increased breakdown of fat (lipolysis) for energy, resulting in the production of ketones.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose and ketone body kinetics in diabetic ketoacidosis.
evidence.snippet: Insulin deficiency results in increased rates of lipolysis and provides increased substrate (free fatty acids) for ketogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: The article points out the signs and symptoms to be aware of when the person is in the acute state of diabetic ketoacidosis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Significance of Insulin Peptide-specific Interferon-γ-related Immune Responses in Ketosis-prone Type 2 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: the involvement of B:9-23rPep-specific IFN-gamma-related immunoreactivity in the pathophysiology of some unprovoked A-beta+ KPD.

nodes.description: Loss of beta cells leads to a deficiency of insulin, a hormone required for glucose uptake.

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) is an endocrine disorder in which pancreatic beta cells stop producing insulin, typically due to autoimmune destruction.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of prediabetes.
evidence.snippet: Insulin resistance and defective glucose sensing at the beta-cell are the central pathophysiologic determinants that together cause hyperglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetic hyperglycemia: link to impaired glucose transport in pancreatic beta cells.
evidence.snippet: Glucose uptake into pancreatic beta cells by means of the glucose transporter GLUT-2, which has a high Michaelis constant, is essential for the normal insulin secretory response to hyperglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: [Hepatogenic diabetes].
evidence.snippet: Peripheral insulin-resistance and impairment of the hepatocellular function are two major possible causes of diabetes mellitus in liver cirrhosis.

evidence.reference_title: The accelerator hypothesis: weight gain as the missing link between Type I and Type II diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Loss of blood glucose control might result from failure of the beta cells to secrete insulin, resistance of the tissues to its action, or a combination of both.

nodes.description: Type I and type II interferons upregulate antigen presentation and chemokine production in beta cells while downregulating oxidative phosphorylation, increasing immunogenicity and metabolic stress.

biological_processes: antigen processing and presentation of peptide antigen via MHC class I

evidence.reference_title: Interferons are key cytokines acting on pancreatic islets in type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The IFN-induced gene signatures have a strong correlation with those observed in beta cells from individuals with type 1 diabetes, and the level of expression of specific IFN-stimulated genes is positively correlated with proteins present in islets of these individuals, regulating beta cell responses to 'danger signals' such as viral infections.

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Symptoms include polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss.

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Symptoms include polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of blood glucose concentration on osmoregulation in diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Poorly controlled insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus is associated with considerable elevations of plasma vasopressin concentrations, although well-controlled diabetics have normal osmoregulated thirst and vasopressin release.

evidence.reference_title: The broad clinical phenotype of Type 1 diabetes at presentation.
evidence.snippet: Immune-mediated (auto-immune) Type 1 diabetes mellitus is not a homogenous entity, but nonetheless has distinctive characteristics. In children, it may present with classical insulin deficiency and ketoacidosis at disease onset, whereas autoimmune diabetes in adults may not always be insulin dependent.

evidence.reference_title: The accelerator hypothesis: weight gain as the missing link between Type I and Type II diabetes.
Can type 1 diabetes be an unexpected complication of obesity?
evidence.snippet: The risk factors of weight gain included using exogenous insulin, intensifying insulin therapy, fear of hypoglycemia and related decrease in physical activity, and psychological factors, such as emotional eating and binge eating
